insight: DRL architect decomposition: 7 domain epics + 1 IV is the right granularity for a pnpm CLI tool that is primarily a template package with a thin Go wrapper. The Go binary fork is foundation (blocks all), template content (skills, agents, commands) forms the bulk of novel work. Advisory fleet unanimously flagged: define inter-epic contracts upfront (agent names, skill paths, phase names, hook events) to enable parallel development.

DRL distribution: ship Go binary via PyPI (Python wheels) instead of pnpm (npm). Researchers have Python, not Node.js. Same pattern as ruff/uv (Rust binaries on PyPI). Go binary unchanged, only packaging wrapper is Python. User confirmed this decision 2026-04-02.

DRL setup tiers: three levels of drl setup to protect researcher flavor customizations. Base (no flag) updates infrastructure only (hooks, config, scaffolding, commands). --core-skill adds structural workflow skills + core agents. --all-skill does full overwrite including style skills/agents. First install always ships everything. Most agents are style-sensitive (field-dependent). Commands always update (thin wrappers). This enables researchers to receive framework updates without losing their field-specific customizations.

When forking a Go binary (ca -> drl), the env var used for repo root override (COMPOUND_AGENT_ROOT) is easily missed during bulk renames because it appears in ~90+ test files. Always grep for env var names separately from binary/command renames - they are a distinct user-facing API surface.

For Go binary forks distributed via PyPI, the tiered setup system (infrastructure/core/style) should detect .claude/ existence BEFORE initDirectories() creates it. The ordering of detection vs creation is critical - checking after creation makes every repo look 'existing'.

npm-specific test packages (npmdist, goreleaser) must be removed when switching distribution from npm to PyPI. These tests reference package.json and .goreleaser.yml that don't exist in the PyPI model and will fail silently or noisily depending on test structure.

Shell hook scripts that resolve REPO_ROOT from dirname should always provide an env var override (e.g. DRL_REPO_ROOT) for test isolation. Without this, tests running from tmpdir cannot control the script's path resolution and will read/write the real repo's state files instead of test fixtures.

Never interpolate shell variables directly into Python inline code (python3 -c). Use sys.argv[1] and pass the value as a script argument instead. Shell variable interpolation into Python source is a code injection vector even when the variable is hardcoded today.

When Go CLI needs Python library functionality (e.g., PDF extraction via PyMuPDF), use subprocess calls with JSON output. The Go binary calls 'python -m module.path --json <args>' and parses stdout. This keeps the Go binary self-contained while leveraging Python's ecosystem.

Always add build output directories (go/dist/, dist/) and agent cache paths (**/.claude/.cache/) to .gitignore before committing. The auto-commit from compound-agent can easily include 200MB+ of binaries if gitignore is incomplete.

When creating new agent definition files in .claude/agents/drl/, always update AGENTS.md role table to match. The docs_examples_sync verification evidence requires alignment between agent file frontmatter names and the documented roles. Review caught this as P1 when 5 of 8 agents were missing from AGENTS.md.

Research-specific skill files must avoid software engineering jargon. Terms like 'DDD bounded contexts', 'refactor', 'deploy', 'microservice' leak easily when adapting compound-agent patterns for research. Add both positive (research terms present) and negative (dev terms absent) vocabulary tests.

Epic 7 /drl:* commands use thin-wrapper pattern identical to compound:* -- YAML frontmatter (name with drl: prefix, description, argument-hint) + $ARGUMENTS + mandatory read instruction pointing to .claude/skills/drl/{skill-dir}/SKILL.md. Write permissions to .claude/commands/ may require bypassPermissions agent mode.
